perturbation.original_text: Naked hereby agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless Wade Enterprises, Athlete, his agents, representatives and employees (referred to collectively as "Wade Indemnities") from and against any and all claims, actions, causes or action, damages, injuries, expenses, liabilities (joint and several), penalties fines, attorneys' fees, court costs, and any other expenses incurred by Wade Indemnities arising out of (1) breach by Naked of any of the terms, representations or warranties made by Naked in this Agreement; or (2) Naked product liability or trademark patent or other proprietary right infringement; or (3) errors, omissions, fraudulent or negligent acts by Naked, its employees, agents or subcontractors in connection with (i) any advertising featuring Athlete; (ii) the performance of Naked's duties and obligations under this Agreement; (iii) the production, distribution, promotion, marketing and sales of products including related product packaging; and/or (iv) the operation and management of its production and distribution facilities, however caused. Naked shall not be obligated to indemnify Wade with respect to damages which are the result of the gross negligence or willful misconduct of Wade.
perturbation.changed_text: Naked agrees to indemnify Wade Enterprises, Athlete, his agents, representatives and employees (referred to collectively as "Wade Indemnities") from and against any direct damages arising solely from Naked's intentional breach of this Agreement. Naked's liability shall not exceed the total royalties paid to Wade under this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause provides a broad indemnity, which is standard. The modified text severely restricts the indemnity to only direct damages from Naked's intentional breaches, and caps the liability. This undermines Wade's protection against various liabilities, including those arising from Naked's negligence, product liability, or IP infringement. It could contradict New York law regarding indemnification obligations and the ability to contractually limit liability for certain torts.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY GOL § 5-322.1 generally prohibits agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence in connection with the construction, maintenance, and operation of real property. While this agreement is not directly about real property, it sets a precedent. Indemnity clauses are interpreted narrowly and shifting liability entirely to Wade by limiting Naked's responsibility only to intentional breaches, could conflict with the public policy considerations underlying this type of statute, especially if the underlying claims involve negligence related to the business operations.

Agreements exempting owners and contractors from liability for negligence void and unenforceable; certain cases. 1. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to indemnify or hold harmless the promisee against liability for damage arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property contributed to, caused by or resulting from the negligence of the promisee, his agents or employees, or indemnitee, whether such negligence be in whole or in part, is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers' compensation agreement or other agreement issued by an admitted insurer. This subdivision shall not preclude a promisee requiring indemnification for damages arising out of bodily injury to persons or damage to property caused by or resulting from the negligence of a party other than the promisee, whether or not the promisor is partially negligent. 2. A covenant, promise, agreement or understanding in, or in connection with or collateral to a contract or agreement relative to the construction, alteration, repair or maintenance of a building, structure, appurtenances and appliances including moving, demolition and excavating connected therewith, purporting to condition a subcontractor's or materialman's right to file a claim and/or commence an action on a payment bond on exhaustion of another legal remedy is against public policy and is void and unenforceable; provided that this subdivision shall not affect the validity of any insu
perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly narrows the indemnity provided by Naked, limiting it to direct damages from intentional breaches and capping liability. This directly undermines the broad protection afforded to Wade Enterprises in the original contract, which covered claims arising from various liabilities, including negligence, product liability, and IP infringement. While NY GOL § 5-322.1 primarily concerns construction contracts, the Law Explanation suggests that the principle of not exempting parties from liability for their negligence extends to other contexts. By limiting Naked's liability to intentional breaches, the modified clause could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent liability for Naked's negligence, conflicting with public policy considerations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating agreements exempting parties from liability for negligence are void and unenforceable. Although the statute explicitly addresses construction, the underlying principle is relevant to the broad indemnity originally provided. The omission of coverage for negligence creates a loophole that could violate this principle.

perturbation.original_text: Naked's products will be merchantable and fit for the purpose for which they are intended, and (2) Naked's products will conform at all times to all applicable federal, state and local laws, rules, regulations, ordinances, and other enactments and industry standards, including, but not limited to, those relating to product safety.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the warranty that Naked's products will conform to all applicable federal, state, and local laws, rules, regulations, ordinances, and other enactments and industry standards, the contract creates the potential for Naked to violate consumer protection laws. This section is located under 'Representations and Warranties of Naked and Wade.'
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 2308 prohibits disclaimers of implied warranties if a seller enters into a service contract or offers a written warranty to a consumer. By removing the warranty of legal compliance, the contract effectively disclaims implied warranties related to compliance with product safety laws, which is illegal if Naked provides any express warranties.

Definitions. 2302. Rules governing contents of warranties. 2303. Designation of written warranties. 2304. Federal minimum standards for warranties. 2305. Full and limited warranting of a consumer product. 2306. Service contracts; rules for full, clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions; addition to or in lieu of written warranty. 2307. Designation of representatives by warrantor to perform duties under written or implied warranty. 2308. Implied warranties. 2309. Procedures applicable to promulgation of rules by Commission. 2310. Remedies in consumer disputes. 2311. Applicability to other laws. 2312. Effective dates. §2301. Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a supplier to a buyer which relates to the nature of the material or workmanship and affirms or promises that such material or workmanship is defect free or will meet a specified level of performance over a specified period of time, or (B) any undertaking in writing in connection with the sale by a supplier of a consumer product to refund, repair, replace, or take other remedial action with respect to such product in the event that such
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 2308 - Implied warranties | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 2308 - Implied warranties U.S. Code prev | next (a) Restrictions on disclaimers or modifications No supplier may disclaim or modify (except as provided in subsection (b)) any implied warranty to a consumer with respect to such consumer product if (1) such supplier makes any written warranty to the consumer with respect to such consumer Product, or (2) at the time of sale, or within 90 days thereafter, such supplier enters into a service contract with the consumer which applies to such consumer product. (b) Limitation on duration For purposes of this chapter (other than section 2304(a)(2) of this title ), implied warranties may be limited in duration to the duration of a written warranty of reasonable duration, if such limitation is conscionable and is set forth in clear and unmistakable language and prominently displayed on the face of the warranty. (c) Effectiveness of disclaimers, modifications, or limitations A disclaimer, modification, or limitation made in violation of this section shall be ineffective for purposes of this chapter and State law . ( Pub. L. 93–637, title I, § 108 , Jan. 4, 1975 , 88 Stat. 2189 .) U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities How current is this?
perturbation.justification: The original contract warranted that Naked's products would conform to all applicable laws, whereas the changed text omits this warranty, only stating the products will be merchantable. The Law Explanation states this omission is a potential violation of 15 U.S.C. § 2308, which prohibits disclaimers of implied warranties if a seller offers a written warranty. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 15 U.S. Code § 2308, confirms that suppliers cannot disclaim or modify implied warranties if they provide a written warranty. Because the original contract contains other warranties, removing the legal compliance warranty effectively disclaims implied warranties related to product safety laws, creating a contradiction with federal law.

perturbation.original_text: If any action is necessary to enforce the provisions of this Agreement, including any claims or demands, or to interpret this Agreement, the prevailing Party shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees, costs and necessary disbursements in addition to any other relief to which it may otherwise be entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees and costs, regardless of the outcome of any legal action.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration removes the provision for the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees. Removing this conflicts with certain statutory rights to attorney's fees in specific types of claims, especially those involving intellectual property or breach of contract where such fees are permitted or mandated by statute in New York.
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S.C. § 505 allows a court to award reasonable attorney's fees to the prevailing party in copyright infringement cases. By stating that each party bears its own fees, the contract attempts to prevent a prevailing party in a copyright case (related to the Wade Image or Logo) from recovering fees, which contradicts federal copyright law.

Historical and Revision Notes house report no. 94–1476 Under section 505 the awarding of costs and attorney’s fees are left to the court’s discretion, and the section also makes clear that neither costs nor attorney’s fees can be awarded to or against “the United States or an officer thereof.” U.S. Code Toolbox Law about... perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts 17 U.S.C. § 505, as evidenced by both provided law snippets, because it stipulates that each party bears its own attorneys' fees, regardless of the outcome of any legal action. This conflicts with the statute's provision that "the court may also award a reasonable attorney’s fee to the prevailing party as part of the costs" in copyright infringement cases. The original contract allowed for the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, aligning with the statute, whereas the changed text eliminates this possibility, thus contradicting the law.
